Easy: What does being young mean to you, and how does it shape the way you see the world around you?
If you could spend one day with any young trailblazer in history, who would it be and what would you ask them?
What is one lesson from your childhood that you carry with you every single day?
How do you define success at your age, and has that definition changed as you have grown?
What is something unique about your generation that you wish the world celebrated more?
Who in your life has shown you what it looks like to grow up with grace and purpose?
What is a simple habit or ritual that helps you stay grounded during the chaos of growing up?
How do you find joy in the in-between moments of life — the ones that do not make the highlight reel?
What does it look like to be kind to yourself on the days when growing up feels overwhelming?
If your younger self could see you now, what do you think would make her the most proud?
What is one expectation placed on young people today that you believe deserves to be challenged?
How do you define confidence, and what has helped you build it throughout your journey?
What role does friendship play in helping young people navigate the challenges of growing up?
What is a fear you faced as a young person that turned out to make you stronger on the other side?
How do you stay connected to your values when the world seems to be constantly pulling you in different directions?
What does authentic self-expression look like for a young person in today's world?
If you could leave one message for every young girl who feels like she does not belong, what would it be?
How has your community shaped who you are becoming as a young woman?
What is something you love about being your age that you never want to take for granted?
How do you think about the relationship between growing up and staying curious?
What does it feel like to stand in your power as a young woman, and how did you find that power?
What is one thing you wish adults understood better about the experience of being young today?
How do you nurture your mental and emotional well-being during some of the most formative years of your life?
What does resilience look like for a young person, and when did you first discover yours?
How do you use your everyday choices as a young person to reflect the values you want to be known for?
Medium: How can young people leverage their unique perspective to solve problems that older generations have failed to address?
What does it mean to build a legacy before you have even fully figured out who you are?
How do you cultivate leadership in yourself when no one has officially given you permission to lead?
What role does mentorship play in helping young people transform their dreams into meaningful action?
How can a young person turn a personal struggle into a platform for collective healing?
What does it look like to lead with empathy in spaces that were not originally designed to include you?
How do you decide which causes are worth your time, energy, and voice as a young advocate?
In what ways can young people hold institutions accountable without losing access to the seats at the table they have worked so hard to earn?
What does safe failure actually look like, and how can communities better support young people through it?
How do we redefine what a young leader looks like so that more young people see themselves in that role?
What is something young people are doing right now to change the world that deserves far more attention than it receives?
How can young women use their platform — no matter how large or small — to lift other young women up?
What is the connection between a young person's personal story and their capacity to lead with authenticity?
How do we make space for young people to change their minds and evolve without being defined by who they used to be?
What does it mean to build community across differences as a young person in a divided world?
How can young people develop the discipline of purpose without sacrificing the freedom of exploration?
What is an issue affecting youth in your community that rarely makes national headlines, and why does it matter?
How do you stay motivated to lead and serve when results take longer to appear than your patience can hold?
What does it mean to be a young person who leads from a place of service rather than ego?
How can we create mentorship pipelines that reflect the full diversity of young people's backgrounds and ambitions?
What is one truth about the youth experience that adults in power often misunderstand or romanticize?
How do young people balance the urgency of the issues they care about with the long-game thinking that lasting change requires?
What does economic opportunity look like for young people, and how do we make it more equitable?
How can schools and communities better prepare young people not just for careers, but for full, meaningful lives?
What would you say to a young person who has a vision for change but feels too young to be taken seriously?
Hard: How do we dismantle the systems that continue to limit the potential of young people before they ever have the chance to discover it?
What would a truly youth-centered policy agenda look like, and who needs to be in the room to build it?
How do we ensure that the energy and idealism of young activists is not exploited by institutions seeking credibility without offering real change?
What responsibility do those of us who have access, education, and opportunity carry in dismantling barriers for those who do not?
How do we move beyond token youth representation in spaces of power and toward genuine shared decision-making?
What does intergenerational justice demand of the adults who are making decisions today that young people will live with for decades?
How do we reconcile the urgency young people feel about the state of the world with the slow, grinding pace of institutional change?
In what ways does the education system need to be reimagined to truly serve the young people who will inherit the most complex world in human history?
How do we build a culture that values the wisdom of young people not as a novelty but as a necessary and indispensable resource?
What does it mean to advocate for equity when the very systems you are working within were built to preserve inequality?
How do we protect the mental health of young activists who carry the weight of the world's most pressing crises on their shoulders?
What is the difference between empowering young people and burdening them with problems that adults created and refuse to solve?
How can communities create lasting infrastructure that allows young people to lead beyond the moment of inspiration, into the work of sustained change?
What does it look like to build cross-generational coalitions where neither youth nor elders are expected to diminish themselves to collaborate?
How do we address the ways that race, class, and geography compound the challenges that young people face, making equity not just a goal but an obligation?
What systemic investments would you prioritize to ensure that every young person has access to mental health support, not as a privilege but as a right?
How do we hold media and technology companies accountable for the environments they create for young people who are still forming their identities?
What does it mean to lead a movement with integrity when the pressure to perform progress is louder than the call to create it?
How do we prepare young people to navigate a world in which artificial intelligence is reshaping the very nature of work, creativity, and human connection?
What does climate justice mean to the generation that will spend the majority of their lives living with decisions already made?
How do we build educational and civic institutions that are designed to evolve alongside the young people they are meant to serve?
What does it mean to pass the torch of leadership without abandoning the young people you hand it to?
How do we create accountability structures that prevent the burnout and disillusionment of the young leaders we so desperately need?
In a world where information moves faster than wisdom, how do we equip young people with the discernment to lead with truth and integrity?
What would it look like to truly invest in youth not as the leaders of tomorrow, but as the essential voices and agents of change we need right now?
